---
title: "Absent from Chronic"
subtitle: "Predictors of Work Absenteeism Associated with Chronic Conditions Among Canadian Workers"
---

![Distribution of total workdays absent in the past 3 months across the employed Canadian population (n = 63,843). Over 70% of workers report zero absent days; the distribution is heavily right-skewed with a maximum of 63 days observed.](images/g01_lop_days_histogram.png){width=90%}

## Welcome

This site documents the statistical analysis replication for the study *"Predictors of Work Absenteeism Associated with Chronic Conditions Among Canadian Workers"* (Kermiche, Blanchette, & Laprise, 2025). The analysis draws on two pooled cycles of the Canadian Community Health Survey (CCHS 2010–2011 and 2013–2014), producing an analytic sample of 63,843 employed respondents representing approximately 17.8 million Canadian workers.

The site is organized into four sections:

- **Project** — The statistical analysis instructions that define the study's requirements (§1–§6), including variable selection, sample construction, and the full analytic plan.
- **Pipeline** — Technical documentation of the data pipeline that transforms raw CCHS SPSS files into analysis-ready Parquet datasets, including the CACHE and INPUT manifests.
- **Data Primer** — Variable traceability (§2.2 requirements vs. implementation) and univariate distribution profiles for all 69 variables in the analytic dataset.
- **Analysis** — Five exploratory reports organized by the Andersen Behavioral Model domains: Outcome (LOP day decomposition), Exposure (17 chronic conditions), Predisposing (socio-demographics), Facilitating (health system and behaviours), and Needs (perceived health and functional limitations).

## Pipeline Architecture

The data flows through four sequential lanes — from raw SPSS files through metadata extraction, full transport (Ferry), cleaning and transformation (Ellis), to automated validation (Test). All outputs are version-controlled and reproducible.

![Pipeline architecture showing the four-lane flow from raw CCHS data through metadata extraction, ferry transport, Ellis transformation, and automated testing.](images/pipeline-architecture.jpg){width=90%}
